title: Language of Chemistry
subtitle: Chapter 1: Chemical Arithmetic & Stoichiometry — NEB Class 11

content.text: 1. Dalton's Atomic Theory
John Dalton (1808) le propose gareko atomic theory ko postulates:
① All matter consists of extremely small, indivisible particles called atoms.
② Atoms of the same element are identical in all respects (mass, size, properties).
③ Atoms of different elements are different and have different properties.
④ Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. (Law of Conservation of Mass)
⑤ Atoms combine in simple whole number ratios to form compounds.
2. Laws of Chemical Combination
2.1 Law of Conservation of Mass
Lavoisier (1774): Matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. Total mass of reactants = Total mass of products.

content.label: 💡 Experiment:
content.text: Landolt's experiment: H-shaped tube ma NaCl ra AgNO₃ solution mix garda white precipitate (AgCl) bancha. Reaction agadi ra pachi ko total mass equal hunxa.

content.name: General Equation
content.note: Applies to all closed system chemical reactions.

content.text: 2.2 Law of Definite (Constant) Proportions
Proust (1799): A pure chemical compound always contains the same elements combined in a fixed proportion by mass, regardless of source or method of preparation.
Example: Water (H₂O) from any source always has H:O mass ratio = 1:8 (2g H : 16g O)

content.label: 🔬 Numerical:
content.text: 5.4g CuO ma 1.08g O bhaye, Cu:O = 4.32:1.08 = 4:1. Arko experiment ma 2.3g CuO ma 1.84g Cu bhaye, O = 0.46g, ratio = 1.84:0.46 = 4:1 → Same ratio!
2.3 Law of Multiple Proportions
Dalton (1803): When two elements form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in simple whole number ratios.
Example: H₂O ra H₂O₂ ma, 2g H sanga combine hunxa: 16g O (water) ra 32g O (hydrogen peroxide). Ratio of O = 16:32 = 1:2 ✓
2.4 Law of Reciprocal Proportions

Richter (1792): When two different elements combine separately with a fixed mass of a third element, the ratio of their masses is either same or simple multiple of the ratio in which they combine with each other.
Example: C + O₂ → CO₂ (12g C : 32g O); 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O (4g H : 32g O). C ra H ko ratio = 12:4 = 3:1. CH₄ ma C:H = 12:4 = 3:1 → Same ratio!
2.5 Gay-Lussac's Law of Combining Volumes
When gases react, they do so in volumes that bear a simple whole number ratio to each other and to the volumes of gaseous products (at same T & P).

content.name: Example: Ammonia Synthesis
content.note: 1L N₂ reacts with 3L H₂ to produce 2L NH₃

content.text: 3. Atomic Weight & Molecular Weight
Atomic Weight: Average mass of an atom of an element compared to 1/12th mass of C-12 isotope.

content.name: Atomic Mass Unit (amu/u)
content.note: Also called unified atomic mass unit (u)

content.text: Molecular Weight: Sum of atomic weights of all atoms in a molecule.
4. Mole Concept
1 mole = Amount of substance containing Avogadro's number (Nₐ) of particles = 6.022 × 10²³ particles

content.name: Key Relations
content.formula: n = \frac{\text{Given mass (g)}}{\text{Molar mass (g/mol)}} = \frac{\text{Number of particles}}{N_A} = \frac{\text{Volume at STP (L)}}{22.4\ \text{L/mol}}
content.note: STP: 0°C, 1 atm; Molar volume of any gas = 22.4 L

content.label: 🧮 Numerical Tips:
content.text:
• 12g C = 1 mole C atoms = 6.022×10²³ atoms
• 18g H₂O = 1 mole H₂O = 6.022×10²³ molecules
• 44g CO₂ at STP occupies 22.4 L
5. Stoichiometry
Calculation of reactants and products in a chemical reaction using balanced equation.
5.1 Mass-Mass Relationship

Q: How much CaO from 10g CaCO₃? 
Ans: (56/100) × 10 = 5.6g CaO
5.2 Mass-Volume Relationship

content.name: Example: Zn + H₂SO₄
content.formula: Zn + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + H_2 \\ 65\text{g} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad 22.4\text{L at STP}
content.note: 65g Zn produces 22.4L H₂ gas at STP

content.text: 5.3 Volume-Volume Relationship

content.name: Example: CH₄ combustion
content.note: 1L CH₄ requires 2L O₂ for complete combustion

content.text: 6. Limiting Reactant
The reactant that is completely consumed first in a reaction, limiting the amount of product formed.

content.label: 🎯 How to find:
content.text:
1. Write balanced equation
2. Convert masses to moles
3. Compare mole ratio with stoichiometric ratio
4. The reactant with lesser ratio is limiting

content.name: Example
content.note: 10.6g Na₂CO₃ = 0.1 mol; 7.3g HCl = 0.2 mol. Ratio required 1:2 → Exact match, no excess!

content.text: 7. Empirical & Molecular Formula

Empirical Formula: Simplest whole number ratio of atoms in a compound.
Molecular Formula: Actual number of atoms in a molecule.

content.name: Relation
content.formula: \text{Molecular Formula} = n \times \text{Empirical Formula} \\ n = \frac{\text{Molecular Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}
content.note: n is always a whole number (1, 2, 3...)

content.label: 📝 Steps to find:
content.text:
1. Convert % composition to grams (assume 100g sample)
2. Convert grams to moles (÷ atomic mass)
3. Divide by smallest mole value → simple ratio
4. Multiply to get whole numbers → Empirical formula
5. Find n = Molecular mass / Empirical mass

Example: Compound with C=26.6%, H=2.2%, O=71.2%, Mol. mass=90
→ Empirical: C₁H₁O₂ (mass=45) → n=90/45=2 → Molecular: C₂H₂O₄
8. Avogadro's Hypothesis
Equal volumes of all gases at same temperature and pressure contain equal number of molecules.

content.name: Mathematical Form
content.note: Basis for determining atomicity and molecular formulas of gases

content.label: 🔬 Application:
content.text:
• Determine atomicity: H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl (1 vol + 1 vol → 2 vol) → H and Cl are diatomic
• Find molecular formula from vapour density: Mol. wt. = 2 × V.D.
9. Vapour Density & Molecular Weight

content.name: Key Formula
content.formula: \text{Vapour Density (V.D.)} = \frac{\text{Mass of certain volume of gas}}{\text{Mass of same volume of } H_2} \\ \text{Molecular Weight} = 2 \times \text{V.D.}

content.text: Example: Gas with V.D. = 22 → Mol. wt. = 44 → Could be CO₂

content.label: 🎓 Exam Important Points:
content.text:
✓ Dalton's theory explains laws of chemical combination
✓ Law of conservation of mass verified by Landolt
✓ Mole concept connects microscopic & macroscopic world
✓ Limiting reactant determines theoretical yield
✓ Empirical formula from % composition; molecular from V.D. or mass spec
✓ Avogadro's law → 22.4 L/mol at STP
